---
title: "Domain Literacy"
subtitle: "The doctrine, standards, and evidence base you need to talk to emergency managers without wasting their time."
---

There is an experiential gulf between emergency managers and researchers. Emergency managers are typically non-technical, and researchers are typically non-operational. That gap complicates technology transfer: academics, operating in good faith, end up researching and building solutions to problems that don't exist in practice.

You don't need to become an emergency manager. You do need to recognize the frameworks they live inside, because those frameworks determine what a deployable system even looks like.

## For your context...

The field is chronically under-resourced — which is exactly why pragmatic, imperfect tools are welcome here.

| Finding | Source |
|---|---|
| 57% of local EM agencies run on **one or fewer** full-time employees | Argonne / NPAC staffing study, 2025 [Link](https://www.anl.gov/dis/npac/EMStudy){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm} |
| 56% of directors are "dual-hatting" (EM is not their only job) | Argonne / NPAC, 2025 [Link](https://www.anl.gov/dis/npac/EMStudy){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm} |
| 15% of local director positions are **unpaid volunteers** | Argonne / NPAC, 2025 [Link](https://www.anl.gov/dis/npac/EMStudy){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm} |
| State EM budgets range \$500K–\$530M; **median under \$10M** | Deloitte–NEMA National Risk Study, 2025 [Link](https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/government-public-sector-services/emergency-management-preparedness-response.html){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm} |
| Agencies want AI but call commercial offerings "not quite there yet" | Deloitte–NEMA, 2025 [Link](https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/government-public-sector-services/emergency-management-preparedness-response.html){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm} |
| Burnout exceeds 50% among emergency-services personnel | Patel et al., 2023 [Link](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37932942/){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm} |

::: {.callout-note}
The takeaway from this table is not "EM is sad." It's that the bar for *useful* is low and the appetite is high — a domain where a "good enough" tool that saves real hours is genuinely valued.
:::

## Operating doctrine

- **NIMS / ICS** — the National Incident Management System and Incident Command System are the standardized frameworks that structure how personnel, resources, and information flow during an incident. The same structures scale from local flooding to multi-state disasters.
    *National Incident Management System*, 3rd ed. (DHS/FEMA, 2017). [Link](https://training.fema.gov/emiweb/is/is700b/handouts/national_incident_management%20system_third%20edition_october_2017.pdf){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}

- **The Whole Community approach** — the doctrine of genuinely engaging community members, especially those most vulnerable to disaster impacts, across all four phases. It emerged from documented disparities in disaster outcomes (e.g., Hurricane Katrina). This is why equity is not an add-on in EM — it's foundational.
    *A Whole Community Approach to Emergency Management* (FEMA, 2011). [Link](https://www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/2023-04/whole-community-approach-to-emergency-management.pdf){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}

## Credentialing to understand the domain

You don't need to get certified to do this research, but working through the right courses is the fastest way to pick up the vocabulary and structure practitioners take for granted.

### Free courses

The entry-level FEMA courses are short (a few hours each), free, and online — a reasonable starting point for any researcher in this space.

- **IS-100** — Introduction to the Incident Command System (ICS 100). [Course](https://training.fema.gov/is/courseoverview.aspx?code=IS-100.c){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **IS-200** — ICS for Single Resources and Initial Action Incidents. [Course](https://training.fema.gov/is/courseoverview.aspx?code=IS-200.c){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **IS-700** — An Introduction to the National Incident Management System (NIMS). [Course](https://training.fema.gov/is/courseoverview.aspx?code=IS-700.b){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **IS-800** — National Response Framework, An Introduction. [Course](https://training.fema.gov/is/courseoverview.aspx?code=IS-800.d){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **IS-5.a** — An Introduction to Hazardous Materials. [Course](https://training.fema.gov/is/courseoverview.aspx?code=IS-5.a){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}

### Commitment required

These go well beyond an afternoon — most run multiple days in person and require hands-on skills practice, not just reading. Most hazard-specific disciplines (hazmat, swiftwater, rope, trench, confined space, etc.) are structured around the same three-tier progression:

- **Awareness** — recognize the hazard, understand the risks, and know when to call for trained help. This is the level a researcher would realistically pursue.
- **Operations** — take defensive action and support a rescue without entering the hazard zone yourself.
- **Technician** — perform the actual rescue or mitigation. This is full operational competency, typically a prerequisite for paid or volunteer response roles.

Each tier builds on the last, and most jurisdictions require Technician-level training before someone can be assigned that role on an incident — these are not credentials to collect casually.

- **HAZWOPER / Hazmat Awareness** — OSHA's hazardous-materials awareness-level training, relevant to any tool touching chemical or industrial incidents. [OSHA overview](https://www.osha.gov/hazwoper){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **Swiftwater Awareness** — recognizing flood and fast-water hazards, relevant to flood-response use cases. [Course](https://teex.org/class/tng80a/){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **Trench Rescue Awareness** — recognizing collapse and excavation hazards. [Course](https://teex.org/class/res120/){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **Rope Rescue Awareness** — recognizing high-angle and rope-access hazards. [Course](https://teex.org/class/xtn098/){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **Confined Space Awareness** — recognizing atmospheric and entrapment hazards in confined spaces. [Course](https://teex.org/class/www352/){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
- **Emergency Medical Responder (EMR)** — entry-level EMS certification; useful background for response-phase use cases. [NREMT overview](https://www.nremt.org/discover/emr){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}
